summary: This PR adds range request support to Flask's `send_file` by passing `accept_ranges=True` and `complete_length` to werkzeug's `make_conditional`, with a `TypeError` fallback for older werkzeug versions and a `close_on_416` guard for file cleanup. The change is generally sound but has several correctness and test-coverage gaps that should be addressed before merging.

findings.title: TypeError catch is too broad and the fallback path is untested
findings.detail: The `except TypeError` block at line 593 is intended to detect older werkzeug versions whose `make_conditional` does not accept `accept_ranges`/`complete_length` kwargs. However, this catch is too broad: any legitimate `TypeError` raised inside `make_conditional` (e.g., due to a programming error or wrong argument type) will be silently swallowed, causing a silent fallback to non-range-aware behavior and potentially masking real bugs. A more targeted approach would be to inspect the signature of `make_conditional` once at import time (e.g., via `inspect.signature`) and branch accordingly, rather than relying on exception-driven dispatch. Furthermore, no test exercises the fallback path — the `test_send_file_range_request` tests are skipped under exactly the conditions where the `TypeError` branch fires (absence of `Range.to_content_range_header`), so the fallback is completely untested.

findings.title: File resource not properly released on 416: rv.close() should be used instead of file.close()
findings.detail: When `close_on_416` is True and `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` is raised, the code calls `file.close()` directly. However, by this point the file has already been wrapped via `wrap_file(request.environ, file)` and embedded in the response object `rv`. Calling `file.close()` directly while `rv` still holds a reference to the wrapped iterator is technically safe only because `rv` is about to be discarded — but it is inconsistent with Flask conventions and may leave the response in an indeterminate state on some WSGI servers that hold onto the wrapper. The correct approach is to call `rv.close()` instead, which will properly propagate closure through the response object and any underlying wrappers. Additionally, there is no test that verifies the file descriptor is actually closed in this path (e.g. using a spy/mock on `file.close` or `rv.close`). A test should be added to confirm resource cleanup on 416.

findings.title: Range request with a file-object (not filename string) not tested
findings.detail: When a file-object is passed directly to `send_file` instead of a filename string, `fsize` remains `None` and `complete_length=None` is passed to `make_conditional`. This edge case — range requests against a file-object — is not covered by any test. The behavior (whether werkzeug handles `complete_length=None` gracefully with range requests) should be verified.

findings.title: close_on_416 flag is fragile and redundant; prefer direct condition or clarifying comment
findings.detail: The `close_on_416` boolean is set to `True` only in the branch where `file` is opened internally (i.e., when a filename string was provided rather than a file-object). At the use site it guards `file.close()` on a 416 response. This flag is essentially a proxy for "did we open the file ourselves?", a condition already implicit from the code structure (and equivalent to `filename is not None`). Using an extra mutable sentinel boolean that must be kept in sync with control flow is fragile. Consider either replacing it with a direct check (`if filename is not None`) at the except block, or at minimum adding an inline comment such as `# we opened the file, so we are responsible for closing it on error`.

findings.title: Range request with conditional=False not tested
findings.detail: The new `accept_ranges=True` path inside `make_conditional` is only reached when `conditional=True`. There is no test verifying that supplying a `Range` header while calling `send_file` with `conditional=False` correctly returns a 200 with full content (i.e., the range is ignored). This guard is important for correctness and should be explicitly covered.

findings.title: X-Sendfile path combined with range requests is untested
findings.detail: When `use_x_sendfile=True` and a `Range` header is present, `close_on_416` remains `False` (Flask never opened the file). If `make_conditional` raises `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`, the exception propagates and the `X-Sendfile` header set on the original `rv` is never sent — which is arguably correct, but has not been analyzed or tested. There is no test covering the combination of `use_x_sendfile=True` and a range request (including out-of-range scenarios) to confirm the expected behavior. While no concrete bug was identified in this path, the lack of coverage means regressions could go undetected.

findings.title: 304 x-sendfile header removal not re-tested after refactor
findings.detail: The `rv.status_code == 304` check that strips the `x-sendfile` header was repositioned as part of this PR. While existing tests cover the 304 conditional response path, no test explicitly verifies that a conditional request returning 304 via the new range-aware code path still correctly removes the `x-sendfile` header.

findings.title: Typo in skip reason string
findings.detail: The reason string reads `"not implement within werkzeug"` which should be `"not implemented in werkzeug"` (or similar grammatically correct phrasing). The surrounding test file uses grammatically correct English strings throughout.

findings.title: Long expression split across lines inconsistently
findings.detail: The `last_modified` assignment spans three lines with a chained method call that is hard to follow. Assigning the path separately (e.g., `path = os.path.join(…)`) and then computing `datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(os.path.getmtime(path)).replace(microsecond=0)` on one line would be more readable and consistent with the rest of the test file.

findings.title: Import continuation style inconsistent with existing code
findings.detail: The new import uses a backslash line continuation to add one extra name, while the existing file-level imports either fit on a single line or use parentheses for wrapping. Consider consolidating onto a single line or switching to parenthesized imports for consistency with the broader Flask codebase style.

findings.title: File leak on RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable when file was already wrapped
findings.detail:
When `close_on_416` is False (i.e., the caller passed in their own file object), `file.close()` is not called on a 416 response. However, the more subtle issue is the opposite: even when `close_on_416` is True and the file was opened internally, `data = wrap_file(request.environ, file)` (line 555) has already wrapped the file in a WSGI file wrapper. The `wrap_file` result is passed into `rv` (the response object). When `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` is raised and re-raised after explicitly calling `file.close()`, the file handle has been closed, but the `rv` response object (which holds a reference to the wrapped file) is abandoned without being closed. This can leak the file descriptor on some WSGI servers that hold on to the wrapper. More critically, on some platforms `wrap_file` may return the raw file object itself, so closing it through `file.close()` is correct, but the abandoned `rv` might still attempt to iterate the already-closed file.

The real correctness bug: `rv = rv.make_conditional(...)` may internally call werkzeug's range-slicing, which seeks into the wrapped file. If `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` is raised, `file.close()` is called directly (line 597). But `data` holds `wrap_file(request.environ, file)` which is already embedded in `rv`. Calling `file.close()` while `rv` still holds a reference to the wrapped file iterator is safe only if `rv` is truly discarded — which it is here since the exception propagates. This is actually okay in practice, but the response `rv` is not explicitly closed before re-raising, which is inconsistent with Flask's convention and may leave the response in an indeterminate state on some WSGI servers. Consider calling `rv.close()` instead of `file.close()` to properly release all resources.

findings.title: x-sendfile header not removed on 416 response; only removed on 304
findings.detail: After the refactor the `rv.headers.pop('x-sendfile', None)` guard only fires when `rv.status_code == 304`. But if `make_conditional` somehow results in a 416 (Requested Range Not Satisfiable), the exception is raised and that check is never reached. That's fine for the 416 case. However, when `use_x_sendfile` is active and `filename` is set, `data` is `None`, `file` is never opened (so `close_on_416` is `False`), and `fsize` is set. If `make_conditional` raises `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` in this path, the exception propagates, which is correct. But there is a deeper issue: when `use_x_sendfile` is True, the code reaches line 545-546 and sets `fsize` and the `X-Sendfile` header. Then it calls `make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=fsize)`. If a range request comes in for an out-of-range byte range, werkzeug raises `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`. This exception propagates out of `send_file` and turns into a 416 HTTP response, but the 416 response body is generated by werkzeug's exception handler — not by the function — so the `X-Sendfile` header from the original `rv` is never sent to the client. This is actually correct behavior. No real bug here, but the x-sendfile path with range requests has not been considered in tests.

findings.title: TypeError catch is too broad: masks real TypeErrors from make_conditional
findings.detail: The `except TypeError` at line 593 is intended to detect that the older werkzeug `make_conditional` signature doesn't accept `accept_ranges` and `complete_length` kwargs. However, if the new werkzeug's `make_conditional` (or any code it calls internally) raises a `TypeError` for a legitimate programming error (e.g., wrong argument type passed to it), that error will be silently swallowed and `send_file` will fall back to calling `rv.make_conditional(request)` without range support. This could mask real bugs. A more targeted approach would be to inspect the signature of `make_conditional` once (e.g., using `inspect.signature` or `getattr` checks) rather than catching `TypeError` broadly.

findings.title: close_on_416 flag is unnecessary and fragile; use filename is not None check instead
findings.detail: `close_on_416` is set to `True` only when the file is opened internally (line 554), i.e., when `filename` was provided (not a file-like object). The same condition can be expressed more clearly and reliably as `filename is not None` at the point of the except block, since `filename` is already None when a file object was passed in. The `close_on_416` flag introduces an extra mutable state variable that must be kept in sync with the control flow, making the code more fragile.

findings.title: Import continuation style inconsistent with existing code
findings.detail: The existing imports in this file use a backslash continuation (e.g., lines 41-42 in the style sample: `from .globals import … \\\n     current_app, request`). However, the new import also uses a backslash but the break point is unusual — the existing style tends to keep the `from … import …` on as few lines as possible or uses parentheses elsewhere. More importantly, the rest of the file (e.g., the `from .globals` import) uses a trailing backslash with the continuation indented to align with the opening name. The new import would be cleaner and more consistent if written as a single line or wrapped with parentheses, since the file's own top-of-file `from werkzeug.exceptions import BadRequest, NotFound` was already a single line that this change splits with a backslash while adding only one more name.

findings.title: `close_on_416` flag is an indirect proxy for a simple condition
findings.detail: `close_on_416` is set to `True` only in the branch where `file` is opened locally (line 554) and is `False` everywhere else. At the use site (line 596-597) it guards `file.close()`. The name is descriptive enough, but the variable is essentially tracking "did we open the file ourselves?", which is already implicit from the code structure. A comment in that branch (e.g. `# we opened the file, so we must close it on error`) might be clearer than an extra boolean flag, bringing the style closer to how the existing code uses inline comments rather than auxiliary sentinel booleans. This is a minor style concern only.

findings.title: Typo in skip reason string
findings.detail: The reason string reads `"not implement within werkzeug"` which should be `"not implemented in werkzeug"` (or similar). The surrounding test file uses grammatically correct English strings throughout.

findings.title: Long expression split across lines inconsistently
findings.detail: The `last_modified` assignment spans three lines with the closing parenthesis of `fromtimestamp(…)` followed by `.replace(` on the same line and then `microsecond=0)` on the next. The existing test file wraps long lines by breaking at a logical operator or after an opening parenthesis; here the chain of method calls makes the break point hard to follow. Assigning the path separately (e.g., `path = os.path.join(…)`) and then `datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(os.path.getmtime(path)).replace(microsecond=0)` on one line would be more readable and consistent.

findings.title: TypeError fallback path (old werkzeug) not tested
findings.detail: The `except TypeError` branch (lines 593-594) falls back to `rv.make_conditional(request)` for werkzeug versions older than 0.12. No test exercises this code path (the entire `test_send_file_range_request` test is skipped when `Range.to_content_range_header` is absent, which is exactly the condition under which the TypeError branch would fire). A test that mocks or monkey-patches the new signature away should be added to verify the fallback works correctly.

findings.title: `close_on_416=True` branch (file.close() on 416) not covered by tests
findings.detail: When `close_on_416` is True and a `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` exception is raised, `file.close()` is called before re-raising. The 416 tests in `test_send_file_range_request` do verify that a 416 response is returned, but since the exception is caught and re-raised at the view level the Flask test client turns it into a 416 response — they do NOT specifically assert that the file was closed. There is no test that verifies the file descriptor is cleaned up (e.g. using a spy/mock on `file.close`). This is a resource-leak risk.

findings.title: Range request with `conditional=False` not tested
findings.detail: The new `make_conditional` call with `accept_ranges=True` is only invoked when `conditional=True`. There is no test that verifies that sending a file with `conditional=False` and a `Range` header correctly ignores the range (i.e. returns 200 with full content), ensuring the guard is respected.

findings.title: X-Sendfile path with range requests not tested
findings.detail: When `use_x_sendfile=True` and a `Range` header is present, `close_on_416` remains `False` (file is not opened by Flask). There is no test covering this combination to confirm behavior (e.g. that a 416 is raised without trying to close a file, or that the X-Sendfile header is handled correctly alongside range semantics).

findings.title: Range request with a file-object (not filename string) not tested
findings.detail: `fsize` is only set when `filename_or_fp` is a string and `use_x_sendfile` is False. When a file-object is passed, `fsize` remains `None` and `accept_ranges`/`complete_length` are passed as `True`/`None`. There is no test exercising range requests when a file-object is supplied directly to `send_file`, so this edge case is uncovered.

findings.title: 304 x-sendfile removal path not re-tested after refactor
findings.detail: The `rv.status_code == 304` check that strips the `x-sendfile` header was moved outside the `if conditional:` block indentation as part of this PR. While existing tests cover the 304 case for non-range conditional requests, no test verifies that a conditional request returning 304 combined with the new range-aware code path still correctly removes the `x-sendfile` header.
